title: C Implementation Data Types Properties
breadcrumbs: Define Operation Parameters in a Signature

C Implementation Data Types Properties

C implementation data types properties explanation.

When you define a parameter for a 3GL callout operation in the signature, and its data type should be numeric, then you have to choose one of the following data types in the drop-down list:

  • char
  • short
  • int
  • long
  • long long
  • char * (length 1 - 32)

For the return value of the operation, there is a similar drop-down list, but without the char * (length 1 - 32) option, if you choose to have the return value be put in $status rather than in the first parameter. This is because $status can contain only a number and not a string.

These data types correspond to data types of the C and C++ programming language. The sizes of these data types are:

  • char
    • 1 byte ( 8 bit)
      Note: Even though the data type is called char, it is actually treated as a number, not as a character, because the Uniface data type is Numeric. If a C function returns an ASCII character, and you need it in your ProcScript code as a character instead of a number, then you can use $string("&#%%CHAR;") to get the character back. Here, CHAR is an example output parameter of type Numeric with C data type char.
  • short
    • 2 bytes (16 bit)
  • int
    • 4 bytes (32 bit)
  • long
    • Windows and iSeries
      • 4 bytes (32 bit)
      • A long is the same as an int
    • Other platforms
      • 8 bytes (64 bit)
      • A long is the same as a long long
  • long long
    • 8 bytes (64 bit)
  • char * (length 1 - 32)
    • The length you specify, which is between 1 and 32.
    • The number is a string in the character set defined for the C implementation in the signature, which defaults to $SYS_CHARSET

If the C function you are making an operation for is using a numeric data type other than the ones listed above, chose the one in the drop-down list that has the same size. For example, if the data type used is __int64, choose long long, or if it is ubyte, choose char, or else if it is unsigned int, choose int.

The data types in the drop-down list are all signed data types. Uniface does not support unsigned data types. However, you can activate C functions that have unsigned parameters or an unsigned return type if you choose the corresponding signed type from the drop-down list. The caveat is that Uniface does not directly support the unsigned values of these parameters or return value. If the value such a function returns or puts in an output parameter is big enough to have its highest bit set, then Uniface will see this as a negative number. Conversely, if a negative value is provided on the activate statement for a parameter that has an unsigned data type in the C function, then that number will become a big positive number inside that C function.
